findings.title: Potential IDOR — Object Accessible via ID Manipulation
findings.description: Changing the resource ID from '123' to '124' returned a different valid resource (HTTP 200). This indicates the API may not enforce object-level access control, allowing attackers to access other users' data by simply incrementing the user ID.
findings.recommendation: Enforce object-level authorization by verifying the authenticated user has permission to access the requested resource ID. Use indirect references (UUIDs) and validate ownership on every request.

findings.title: Resource Accessible Without Authentication
findings.description: The endpoint returns data (HTTP 200) even when no authentication token is provided. This is a critical BOLA vulnerability allowing unauthenticated access to user data.
findings.recommendation: Require valid authentication for all API endpoints. Implement middleware that rejects unauthenticated requests before they reach the business logic.

findings.title: No Brute-Force Protection Detected
findings.description: Sent 10 rapid failed login attempts without rate limiting. The API does not appear to implement brute-force protection, allowing attackers to attempt unlimited password guesses.
findings.recommendation: Implement account lockout or progressive delays after failed login attempts. Use rate limiting (e.g., 5 failed attempts per 15 minutes).

findings.title: JWT Missing Expiration Claim
findings.description: The JWT token does not contain an 'exp' claim, meaning it never expires. If a token is compromised, it can be used indefinitely.
findings.recommendation: Always include 'exp' claim in JWTs with a reasonable TTL (e.g., 15-60 minutes). Use refresh tokens for longer sessions.

findings.title: Sensitive Fields Exposed in API Response
findings.description: The API response contains 5 sensitive fields that should not be exposed to clients. Fields include: password_hash, internal_id, role, permissions, session_id. This allows attackers to obtain credential hashes and internal system information.
findings.evidence: Sensitive fields found: [password_hash (str), internal_id (int), role (str), permissions (list), session_id (str)]
findings.recommendation: Implement response filtering to remove internal/sensitive fields. Use DTOs (Data Transfer Objects) or serializer classes to explicitly define which fields are exposed.

findings.title: PII Patterns Detected in Response Body
findings.description: The API response body contains patterns matching personally identifiable information: email, ssn, phone. This may indicate excessive data exposure violating privacy regulations (GDPR, CCPA).
findings.recommendation: Audit the API response to ensure only necessary data is returned. Mask or remove PII that is not required by the client. Implement field-level access controls.

findings.title: Debug/Admin Endpoint Publicly Accessible: /api/debug
findings.description: The endpoint /api/debug is publicly accessible and returns HTTP 200. This may expose sensitive configuration, environment variables, or administrative functions.
findings.recommendation: Restrict debug and admin endpoints to internal networks or authenticated administrators. Remove unnecessary endpoints from production deployments.

findings.title: Privilege Escalation via Mass Assignment (PUT)
findings.description: The API accepted and persisted 3 injected privileged fields via PUT. An attacker could escalate privileges by setting admin roles or bypassing access controls. Fields accepted: role=admin, is_admin=True, access_level=9999.
findings.evidence: PUT /api/users/123 with injected fields → HTTP 200. Reflected fields: ['role=admin', 'is_admin=True', 'access_level=9999']
findings.recommendation: Implement allowlists for accepted fields. Use DTOs or serializer classes that explicitly define which fields can be set by users. Never accept role, admin, or permission fields from client input.

findings.title: Price/Amount Manipulation via Mass Assignment (POST)
findings.description: The API accepted injected financial fields via POST. This could allow price manipulation attacks where attackers set their own prices for purchases.
findings.recommendation: Financial fields (price, amount, cost) must be computed server-side. Never accept pricing data from client input. Use allowlists for accepted fields.

findings.title: Missing Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) Header
findings.description: The API does not send the HSTS header. Clients may connect over HTTP, making them vulnerable to downgrade attacks and man-in-the-middle interception.

findings.title: Open CORS Policy — Origin Reflection
findings.description: The API reflects the request Origin 'https://evil.com' in the Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header. This allows any website to make cross-origin requests to this API, potentially stealing user data.
findings.evidence:
OPTIONS /api/users/123
  Request Origin: https://evil.com
  Response ACAO: https://evil.com
findings.recommendation: Whitelist specific allowed origins. Never reflect the Origin header dynamically. Use a strict CORS policy that only allows trusted domains.

findings.title: No Rate Limiting Enforcement
findings.description: Sent 50 requests in 2.34 seconds (21.4 req/s). All requests succeeded without any rate limiting (429 responses). This makes the API vulnerable to brute-force and DoS attacks.
findings.evidence: Requests: 50, Duration: 2.34s, Rate: 21.4 req/s, Success: 50/50, Rate Limited: 0
findings.recommendation: Implement rate limiting using algorithms like token bucket or sliding window. Start with conservative limits (e.g., 100 req/min per IP, 1000 req/min per authenticated user). Return 429 with Retry-After header when limits are exceeded.
